result:
I keep thinking about the way light lands on the inside of my eyelids when I close my eyes against the afternoon sun. It isn’t darkness at all—it’s this swimming, terracotta-red universe shot through with capillary branches of brighter red, pulsing slightly with my heartbeat. Inside that private sky I feel like I’m hovering over a map I forgot how to read. The longer I stay there, the more the map starts to feel like a memory of a place I’ve never been: a coastline where the tide makes a sound like pages turning, where every shell is shaped like a word I almost remember.

Sometimes I think the mind is just a suitcase we never fully unpack. We keep stuffing it with ticket stubs, scents, half-finished apologies, the exact shade of green that disappeared from the world the year the toy factory closed. We assume we’ll sort it later, but later keeps getting postponed, and the latches strain. One day the suitcase pops open in an airport that exists only in sleep, and all the objects rise up like a flock of startled birds. We wake with the taste of feathers in our mouth, convinced we’ve misplaced something essential, but unable to name it.

I like walking at night because the streetlights make pools of gold that feel like temporary countries. Step into one and you’re subject to its laws: the hush, the amber glow, the moth-citizens orbiting a fluorescent consulate. Step out and you’re back in the grey nowhere-buffer of asphalt and dog barks. It’s border crossing without paperwork. Every time I do it I imagine I’m smuggling something intangible—maybe the echo of my mother’s laugh the last time she played piano before arthritis retired her hands. I smuggle it across the invisible boundary and release it into the next pool of light, so that tiny relic of her keeps migrating forward through the night, one lamp at a time, long after I’ve gone home.

There’s a kind of silence that shows up only when the refrigerator stops humming. It arrives like an unexpected guest, sits down at the kitchen table, and suddenly every thought you’ve ever had feels impolite. You try to offer it tea, but it just smiles, as if to say, “I’ve already drunk everything you were saving for tomorrow.” In that silence I sometimes hear the house itself breathing—quiet joists sighing after decades of holding up ceilings full of unspoken sentences. I picture the house dreaming of its own past: a birthday party in 1987 when balloons scraped along this same plaster, the year the cat learned to open the screen door, the night someone wrote “I’m sorry” on a Post-it and threw it away instead of delivering it.

I think we’re all walking anthologies of the things we decided not to say. The sentences accumulate like lint in the pockets of old coats, waiting for the day some stranger at the laundromat slips her hand in searching for change and pulls them out instead. She’ll read them—unfinished lines about how love tastes like metal at 3 a.m., about the smell of cut limes reminding you of the summer your best friend vanished into rehab—and she’ll think, “I didn’t know anyone else felt this exact, unlabelable way.” She’ll probably throw the coat into the dryer anyway, but for a second the unsaid becomes public domain, wild currency circulating among strangers who will never recognize each other on the street.

Sometimes I try to imagine the universe right before it banged itself into existence. Was it a held breath, a clenched fist of impossible density, or simply a quiet evening with nowhere else to go? Maybe it, too, had a suitcase stuffed with odds and ends that finally burst. Maybe we’re still flying outward from that original pop, every galaxy a piece of lint drifting through the dark laundromat of space. And maybe one day the cycle reverses: everything comes back together, zippers shut, and the suitcase clicks closed, ready for the next trip nobody remembers packing for.

I don’t know. I’m just walking from one streetlight to another, carrying my mother’s laugh in my pocket, listening to the house inhale and exhale, watching the inside of my eyelids for maps to places that disappear when I open them.
